questions.question: The founder of Jainism is considered to be:
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (Adinatha) is considered the first Tirthankara and founder of Jainism. Mahavira (599–527 BCE) was the 24th and last Tirthankara who revived and systematised Jainism.

questions.question: Mahavira attained Nirvana (Moksha) at:
questions.explanation: Mahavira attained Nirvana at Pawapuri (Pavapuri) in Bihar at the age of 72 (527 BCE). He was born at Kundagrama near Vaishali. He attained Kevala Jnana (omniscience) at Jrimbhikagrama.

questions.question: Jainism is divided into two sects:
questions.explanation: After Mahavira's death, Jainism split into: Digambara ('sky-clad' — monks go naked) and Shvetambara ('white-clad' — monks wear white). The split occurred at the Pataliputra council (c. 300 BCE) due to a famine.

questions.question: Gautama Buddha was born at:
questions.explanation: Siddhartha Gautama was born c. 563 BCE at Lumbini (present-day Nepal) in the Shakya clan. His birth, enlightenment, first sermon, and Mahaparinirvana are the four most sacred events in Buddhism.

questions.question: Buddha attained enlightenment (Nirvana/Bodhi) at:
questions.explanation: Siddhartha attained enlightenment at Bodh Gaya (formerly Uruvela/Gaya), Bihar, under the Peepal tree (Bodhi tree) on the bank of the Niranjana river at the age of 35.

questions.question: The first sermon of the Buddha is called:
options: Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta (Turning of the Wheel of Dharma)
questions.explanation: The Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta (Setting the Wheel of Dharma in Motion) was the first sermon delivered at Sarnath (Deer Park/Isipatana) to the five ascetics. This event is called 'Dharma-chakra-pravartana'.

questions.question: The Buddhist scriptures are collectively called:
options: Tipitaka (Three Baskets) — Vinaya, Sutta, Abhidhamma Pitakas
questions.explanation: Tipitaka (Pali) or Tripitaka (Sanskrit) = Three Baskets: Vinaya Pitaka (monastic rules), Sutta Pitaka (discourses of Buddha), Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophical analysis). Written in Pali.

questions.question: The Gandhara art style was a blend of:
questions.explanation: Gandhara art (1st–5th century CE) blended Hellenistic/Greek realism with Buddhist iconography. It emerged in the Gandhara region (Afghanistan/Pakistan). First human images of the Buddha were made here.

questions.question: The concept of 'Ahimsa' was first emphasized in:
questions.explanation: Ahimsa (non-violence) was most strongly and systematically emphasized in Jainism, especially by Mahavira. It became central to Jain philosophy.

questions.question: The first Tirthankara of Jainism is:
questions.explanation: Rishabhadeva (Adinatha) is the first Tirthankara (ford-maker) of Jainism. There are 24 Tirthankaras in total.

questions.question: Mahavira attained 'Kaivalya' (enlightenment) at:
questions.explanation: Mahavira attained Kaivalya (perfect knowledge) at Jrimbhikagrama on the banks of Rijupalika river after 12.5 years of intense meditation.

questions.question: The principle of 'Anekantavada' is associated with:
questions.explanation: Anekantavada (many-sidedness of truth) is a Jain doctrine stating that truth and reality are complex, and no single perspective is complete.

questions.question: Jains were divided into Digambara and Shvetambara at a council held at:
questions.explanation: The split between Digambara (sky-clad, don't wear clothes) and Shvetambara (white-robed) Jains occurred at a council at Pataliputra around 300 BCE.

questions.question: The First Buddhist Council was held at:
questions.explanation: First Buddhist Council (483 BCE) at Rajgriha (Sattapanni Cave), immediately after Buddha's death, presided over by Mahakassapa, compiled Vinaya Pitaka and Sutta Pitaka.

questions.question: 'Dhammachakkapavattana Sutta' refers to:
questions.explanation: Dhammachakkapavattana Sutta is the record of Buddha's first sermon at Sarnath (Deer Park) to his five former companions — the 'Setting in Motion of the Wheel of Dharma'.

questions.question: Bodhgaya is associated with:
questions.explanation: Bodhgaya (Bihar) is where Siddhartha Gautama attained enlightenment under the Bodhi tree and became the Buddha. It is the holiest Buddhist pilgrimage site.

questions.question: Which Ashokan edict describes his conversion to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Bhabru (Calcutta-Bairat) edict mentions Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism and his faith in the Triple Jewel (Buddha, Dhamma, Sangha).

questions.question: 'Silappadikaram' was composed by:
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram (Story of the Anklet) was composed by Ilanko Adigal, a Jain or Buddhist monk who was reportedly a prince of the Chera dynasty.

questions.question: Ellora caves contain temples of:
questions.explanation: Ellora Caves (Maharashtra) contain 34 caves — 12 Buddhist (caves 1-12), 17 Hindu (caves 13-29), 5 Jain (caves 30-34). Shows religious coexistence during 6th-11th centuries CE.

questions.question: India's first nuclear test was conducted at:
questions.explanation: India conducted its first nuclear test (Smiling Buddha) at Pokhran, Rajasthan on 18 May 1974. Second nuclear tests (Pokhran-II/Operation Shakti) were conducted in May 1998.

questions.question: 'Gandharva' art style is known for depicting:
questions.explanation: Gandhara art (1st-5th century CE, NW India and Afghanistan) shows a fusion of Hellenistic/Roman and Indian Buddhist art — the first realistic images of the Buddha in human form.

questions.question: Mathura art school is known for:
options: Indigenous Indian Buddhist images in red sandstone
questions.explanation: Mathura school (1st-12th century CE) produced purely Indian Buddhist, Jain, and Hindu sculptures in red sandstone — without Hellenistic influence, unlike Gandhara school.

questions.question: Mahendravarman I was a Pallava king known for:
options: Pioneering rock-cut cave temples and also writing 'Mattavilasa Prahasana' (a Sanskrit comedy)
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (600–630 CE) pioneered Pallava cave architecture (Mandagapattu, Mahendravadi). He also wrote the Sanskrit play 'Mattavilasa Prahasana' (farce about a drunk Shaivite monk). Initially a Jain who converted to Shaivism.

questions.question: The Mahendravarman I of Pallavas was initially a follower of:
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (600-630 CE) was initially a Jain, converted to Shaivism under the influence of Shaivite saint Appar (Tirunavukkarasar), and became a great Shaiva devotee.

questions.question: The 'Din-i-Ilahi' was founded by:
questions.explanation: Din-i-Ilahi (Divine Faith) was founded by Akbar in 1582 CE — a syncretic religious movement incorporating elements of Hinduism, Islam, Zoroastrianism, Jainism, and Christianity.

questions.question: Which Jain Tirthankara is associated with the symbol of a lion?
questions.explanation: Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara, is symbolized by a lion.

questions.question: The first Buddhist Council was held at which location?
questions.explanation: The first Buddhist Council was convened at Rajgriha (Rajgir) shortly after Buddha's death.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler embraced Jainism and is said to have starved himself to death?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya adopted Jainism and performed Sallekhana (fast unto death) at Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: The term 'Tripitaka' is associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: Tripitaka (Three Baskets) are the canonical texts of Buddhism, comprising Vinaya, Sutta, and Abhidhamma Pitakas.

questions.question: Which Chola king is known for his patronage of Jainism and built the Jain temple at Tirumalai?
questions.explanation: Kulottunga I (later Chola) was a patron of Jainism and constructed the Jain cave temple at Tirumalai.

questions.question: The concept of 'Syadvada' or 'Anekantavada' is a core doctrine of which religion?
questions.explanation: Syadvada (theory of conditional predication) and Anekantavada (non-absolutism) are key Jain philosophical concepts.

questions.question: Which Pallava king was a noted patron of Jainism and authored the work 'Mattavilasa Prahasana'?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was initially a Jain patron but later converted to Shaivism; he wrote Mattavilasa Prahasana.

questions.question: The 'Maha Bodhi Temple' at Bodh Gaya was built during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: Emperor Ashoka built the first temple at Bodh Gaya to mark the site of Buddha's enlightenment.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic is considered a classic of Jain literature and was composed by the Jain monk Ilango Adigal?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, though a secular epic, was authored by the Jain prince-turned-monk Ilango Adigal.

questions.question: The 'Fourth Buddhist Council' was held during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Kushana king Kanishka in Kashmir.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered the founder of Jainism in its present form?
questions.explanation: Mahavira is the 24th Tirthankara and is credited with systematizing Jain doctrines.

questions.question: The concept of 'Triratnas' (Three Jewels) in Jainism includes right faith, right knowledge, and which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Triratnas of Jainism are Samyag Darshan (right faith), Samyag Jnana (right knowledge), and Samyag Charitra (right conduct).

questions.question: Which Buddhist council was held during the reign of King Kanishka?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Kanishka in Kashmir, leading to the split into Hinayana and Mahayana.

questions.question: Which Chola king is known for patronizing Buddhism and building a vihara at Nagapattinam?
questions.explanation: Rajendra I built the Kadaram (Sri Vijaya) vihara at Nagapattinam, reflecting his patronage of Buddhism.

questions.question: The Jain text 'Kalpa Sutra' was composed by which scholar?
questions.explanation: Bhadrabahu is traditionally credited with composing the Kalpa Sutra, a key Jain text detailing the lives of Tirthankaras.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with spreading Buddhism to Sri Lanka?
questions.explanation: Ashoka sent his son Mahinda and daughter Sanghamitta to Sri Lanka to propagate Buddhism.

questions.question: The Pallava king Mahendravarman I was initially a patron of which religion before converting to Jainism?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was initially a Jain but later converted to Hinduism (Shaivism) under the influence of Appar.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between Jainism and Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Jainism believes in the eternal soul (jiva), while Buddhism rejects the concept of a permanent soul (anatta).

questions.question: The famous Jain site of Sittannavasal is located in which modern-day Indian state?
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal, known for its Jain cave temple and frescoes, is located in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Who is the founder of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Siddhartha Gautama, known as Gautama Buddha, founded Buddhism in the 6th century BCE.

questions.question: Which Jain council was held at Vallabhi in the 5th century CE?
questions.explanation: The Second Jain Council at Vallabhi (453 CE) compiled and codified the Jain canonical texts (Siddhanta).

questions.question: What is the collective name of the three main Buddhist canonical texts (Vinaya, Sutta, Abhidhamma)?
questions.explanation: The Tripitaka ('Three Baskets') are the foundational scriptures of Buddhism, comprising Vinaya, Sutta, and Abhidhamma Pitakas.

questions.question: Which Tamil Nadu region is particularly associated with the spread of Buddhism during the Sangam age?
questions.explanation: Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar) was a major centre of Buddhism in early Tamilakam, with Buddhist viharas and missionary activity.

questions.question: Which Jain monk is said to have influenced Chandragupta Maurya to adopt Jainism?
questions.explanation: According to Jain tradition, Bhadrabahu was a revered Jain monk who guided Chandragupta Maurya towards Jain asceticism.

questions.question: Under which Kushan ruler was the Fourth Buddhist Council held?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under Kanishka in the 1st–2nd century CE, likely in Kashmir, leading to the division of Buddhism into Hinayana and Mahayana.

questions.question: What is the Jain doctrine of non-absolutism called?
questions.explanation: Anekantavada emphasizes that reality can be perceived from multiple viewpoints, a key Jain philosophical concept.

questions.question: The Buddhist texts in Pali were first written down in which country?
questions.explanation: The Pali Tripitaka was first committed to writing in Sri Lanka during the 1st century BCE at the Aluvihara monastery.

questions.question: Which Jain text, authored by Bhadrabahu, contains the biographies of the 24 Tirthankaras?
questions.explanation: The Kalpasutra, traditionally attributed to Bhadrabahu, details the lives of the Jain Tirthankaras, especially Mahavira.

questions.question: Who is credited with building the Great Stupa at Sanchi?
questions.explanation: The original brick stupa at Sanchi was commissioned by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE, later expanded.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor embraced Jainism and is said to have starved to death at Shravanabelagola?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya, under the influence of Bhadrabahu, adopted Jainism and fasted unto death.

questions.question: The term 'Triratna' in Jainism refers to which three principles?
options: Right faith, right knowledge, right conduct
questions.explanation: Triratna (Three Jewels) in Jainism are Samyak Darshana, Samyak Jnana, and Samyak Charitra.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for his patronage of Jainism and the construction of the Jain cave temple at Sittannavasal?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I, initially a Jain patron, built the Sittannavasal Jain cave temple before converting to Shaivism.

questions.question: The Buddhist text 'Milinda Panha' records a dialogue between King Menander and which Buddhist monk?
questions.explanation: The Milinda Panha is a dialogue between Indo-Greek king Menander and the Buddhist sage Nagasena.

questions.question: Which Chola king is credited with persecuting the Jain monk and author of 'Jivaka Chintamani'?
questions.explanation: Kulothunga I (Chola) is traditionally associated with the persecution of the Jain monk who wrote Jivaka Chintamani.

questions.question: The concept of 'Sunyata' (emptiness) is a core doctrine of which Buddhist school?
questions.explanation: Sunyata is a central concept in Mahayana Buddhism, especially in the Madhyamaka school founded by Nagarjuna.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic is considered a masterpiece of Jain literature and was composed by the Jain monk Ilango Adigal?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, though a secular epic, was authored by the Jain prince-turned-monk Ilango Adigal.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, which led to the split into Hinayana and Mahayana, was held during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Kushana emperor Kanishka in Kashmir.

questions.question: Which of the following rulers is associated with the propagation of Jainism in the Tamil region?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I, a Pallava king, initially followed Jainism and later converted to Shaivism, but his early patronage helped Jainism in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which Jain text is considered the oldest and most authoritative?
questions.explanation: The Angas are the primary canonical texts of Jainism, believed to contain the direct teachings of Mahavira.

questions.question: The concept of 'Triratna' in Jainism refers to which three jewels?
options: A. Right faith, right knowledge, right conduct
questions.explanation: Triratna (Three Jewels) in Jainism are samyag darshan (right faith), samyag jnana (right knowledge), and samyag charitra (right conduct).

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor embraced Buddhism and sent missionaries to various regions?
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War and propagated it through missionaries as recorded in his edicts.

questions.question: The Jain community in Tamil Nadu is particularly associated with which historical period?
questions.explanation: During the Kalabhra interregnum (3rd–6th century CE), Jainism flourished in Tamil Nadu under royal patronage.

questions.question: Which of the following Buddhist councils led to the division of Buddhism into Sthaviravada and Mahasanghika?
questions.explanation: The Second Buddhist Council at Vaishali (c. 383 BCE) saw the first major schism, leading to the Sthaviravada and Mahasanghika schools.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic is associated with Jainism and was composed by the Jain monk Ilango Adigal?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, written by Ilango Adigal (a Jain prince), is a Tamil epic with Jain themes and moral teachings.

questions.question: The term 'Mahavira' literally means:
questions.explanation: Mahavira means 'Great Hero' and is the title given to Vardhamana, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism.

questions.question: Which Chola king is known for patronizing Jainism and building the Jain temple at Tirumalai?
questions.explanation: Parantaka Chola I (907–955 CE) was a patron of Jainism and constructed the Jain temple at Tirumalai in the North Arcot region.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the Tirthankaras in Jainism?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha was the first Tirthankara, Parshvanatha the 23rd, and Mahavira the 24th and last.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, which resulted in the compilation of the Abhidhamma Pitaka, was held during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Kushana ruler Kanishka in Kashmir.

questions.question: Which of the following Tamil Sangam texts is associated with the patronage of Jainism?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai is a Buddhist epic, but Silappadikaram has strong Jain influences; however, Manimekalai is directly linked to Buddhism.

questions.question: The concept of 'Triratna' (Three Jewels) in Jainism includes right faith, right knowledge, and which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Triratna in Jainism are right faith (samyak darshana), right knowledge (samyak jnana), and right conduct (samyak charitra).

questions.question: Which Tamil Buddhist epic, composed by the poet Ilango Adigal, is considered one of the five great epics of Tamil literature?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, authored by Ilango Adigal (a Jain prince), is a Tamil epic with strong Buddhist and Jain influences.

questions.question: The concept of 'Triratna' (Three Jewels) in Jainism refers to:
options: Right Faith, Right Knowledge, Right Conduct
questions.explanation: Triratna or 'Three Jewels' in Jainism are Samyak Darshana (Right Faith), Samyak Jnana (Right Knowledge), and Samyak Charitra (Right Conduct).

questions.question: Which Chola king is historically known for persecuting Jain monks and converting to Saivism, leading to the decline of Jainism in the Tamil region?
questions.explanation: Kulothunga Chola I (also known as Rajendra Chola II) was a persecutor of Jains and a patron of Saivism; his reign saw the massacre of Jain monks in Chidambaram.

questions.question: The first Buddhist Council held at Rajagriha after the death of Buddha was presided over by:
questions.explanation: The first Buddhist Council at Rajagriha was convened by Mahakassapa (Mahakasyapa), with Ananda reciting the Suttas and Upali reciting the Vinaya.

questions.question: Which Pallava king, initially a Jain, was converted to Saivism by the Tamil saint Appar and subsequently destroyed Jain images?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (600-630 CE) was a patron of Jainism before converting to Saivism under the influence of Appar, leading to his persecution of Jains.

questions.question: The Jain monk who accompanied Alexander and wrote the first known biography of Alexander, 'Indica', was:
questions.explanation: Acharya Bhadrabahu, a Jain monk, accompanied Alexander to India and wrote 'Indica', a lost Greek account of Alexander's campaign.

questions.question: Which of the following was a major Buddhist centre of learning and debate in ancient Tamil Nadu, frequently visited by Chinese pilgrims?
questions.explanation: Kanchipuram, a Pallava capital, had a renowned Buddhist monastery (Vihara) visited by Hiuen Tsang and I-Tsing in the 7th century.

questions.question: The term 'Sallekhana' in Jainism refers to:
questions.explanation: Sallekhana (or Santhara) is a Jain practice of voluntary fasting to death, considered a peaceful and spiritual end.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is credited with sending Buddhist missionaries to Sri Lanka and other regions, including his son Mahendra and daughter Sanghamitra?
questions.explanation: Ashoka the Great sent his son Mahendra and daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka to spread Buddhism, marking a key event in Buddhist missionary history.

questions.question: The Jain text 'Kalpasutra', which narrates the lives of the Tirthankaras including Mahavira, was authored by which Jain saint?
questions.explanation: Acharya Bhadrabahu is traditionally credited with composing the 'Kalpasutra', a key Jain scripture detailing the lives of the Tirthankaras.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between Jainism and Buddhism regarding the concept of the soul?
options: A. Jainism believes in the existence of a permanent soul (jiva), while Buddhism rejects the concept of an eternal soul (anatta).
B. Buddhism believes in a permanent soul (atman), while Jainism denies its existence.
C. Both Jainism and Buddhism believe in an eternal soul that transmigrates.
D. Both Jainism and Buddhism deny the existence of a soul.
questions.explanation: Jainism asserts the existence of an eternal soul (jiva), whereas Buddhism's core doctrine is anatta (no permanent soul).

questions.question: The concept of 'Syadvada' or the theory of conditional predication is associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: Syadvada is a key Jain philosophical doctrine emphasizing that truth is relative and can be expressed from multiple viewpoints.

questions.question: Which of the following Tamil literary works is considered a Jain text and provides insights into early Tamil society?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai is a Tamil epic written by the Jain poet Sittalai Sattanar, reflecting Buddhist and Jain influences in early Tamilakam.

questions.question: The Third Buddhist Council, held at Pataliputra under the patronage of Emperor Ashoka, was presided over by which monk?
questions.explanation: Moggaliputta Tissa presided over the Third Buddhist Council, which compiled the Kathavatthu and led to the expulsion of heretical monks.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a common tenet shared by both Jainism and Buddhism?
options: C. Belief in a creator god
questions.explanation: Both Jainism and Buddhism reject the concept of a creator god and focus on ethical living and karma.

questions.question: The term 'Triratna' in Buddhism refers to the Three Jewels. Which of the following correctly lists them?
options: D. Right faith, Right knowledge, Right conduct
questions.explanation: The Triratna (Three Jewels) in Buddhism are Buddha (the enlightened one), Dhamma (his teachings), and Sangha (the monastic community).

questions.question: Which of the following ancient Tamil kingdoms is known to have patronized Jainism, as evidenced by Jain beds and inscriptions in its hills?
questions.explanation: The Pandya kingdom, especially in the Madurai region, has numerous Jain cave shelters and inscriptions, indicating royal patronage.

questions.question: The 'Mahavibhasha' text, a key commentary on the Sarvastivada school, was compiled during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Mahavibhasha was compiled under the patronage of Kushan emperor Kanishka during the Fourth Buddhist Council in Kashmir.

questions.question: Which of the following places in Tamil Nadu is famous for its Jain temples and a colossal statue of the Jain Tirthankara Gommateshwara (Bahubali)?
questions.explanation: Sravanabelagola in Karnataka (not Tamil Nadu) has the famous Gommateshwara statue; however, the question is a distractor. The correct answer is Sravanabelagola, though it is in Karnataka, it is a key Jain site linked to Tamil Jain traditions.

questions.question: The 'Digambara' and 'Svetambara' sects in Jainism primarily differ on which of the following issues?
options: B. The necessity of nudity for monks
questions.explanation: Digambara monks practice nudity as a symbol of renunciation, while Svetambara monks wear white clothes; this is their main doctrinal difference.

questions.question: Which Buddhist council was convened under the patronage of King Kalashoka at Vaishali?
questions.explanation: The Second Buddhist Council was held at Vaishali around 383 BCE to resolve disputes over monastic discipline.

questions.question: The Tamil epic 'Jivakachintamani' is associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: Jivakachintamani is a classical Jain Tamil epic authored by Tiruttakka Divadar.

questions.question: Which Jain principle advocates the theory of conditional predication, also known as 'syadvada'?
questions.explanation: Anekantavada, the Jain doctrine of non-absolutism, is the basis for syadvada (conditional predication).

questions.question: The Kalinga war led to which major shift in the religious policy of Emperor Asoka?
questions.explanation: Deeply remorseful after the Kalinga war, Asoka embraced Buddhism and propagated the Dhamma.

questions.question: Which Pallava king, initially a patron of Jainism, later converted to Shaivism and composed 'Mattavilasa Prahasana'?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was a Jain follower before converting to Shaivism under the influence of Appar (Tirunavukkarasar).

questions.question: Which Tamil Buddhist epic, attributed to the poet Sittthalai Shattanar, narrates the story of a Buddhist nun?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai is a Tamil Buddhist epic that continues the story of Silappadikaram, focusing on the daughter of Kovalan and Madhavi.

questions.question: The first Jain council at Pataliputra was presided over by which acharya?
questions.explanation: The first Jain council (c. 3rd century BCE) was held at Pataliputra under the presidency of Sthulabhadra to compile the Jain canon.

questions.question: Which Chola king provided patronage to the Buddhist vihara at Nagapattinam?
questions.explanation: Rajendra I built the Kadaram (Sri Vijaya) connection and supported the Buddhist vihara at Nagapattinam, known as the Chudamani Vihara.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Triratna' (Three Jewels) in Jainism consists of Samyak Darshan, Samyak Jnana, and Samyak ________.
questions.explanation: The three jewels of Jainism are right faith (darshan), right knowledge (jnana), and right conduct (charitra).

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the Buddhist Councils?
questions.explanation: The First Council was at Rajgriha (483 BCE), Second at Vaishali (383 BCE), Third at Pataliputra (250 BCE), and Fourth at Kashmir (1st century CE).

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with the propagation of Buddhism and the construction of the Sanchi Stupa?
questions.explanation: Ashoka embraced Buddhism after the Kalinga War and built numerous stupas, including the Great Stupa at Sanchi.

questions.question: The term 'Digambara' and 'Svetambara' in Jainism primarily refers to a difference in:
questions.explanation: Digambaras (sky-clad) practice nudity and non-possession, while Svetambaras (white-clad) wear white clothes and allow possession of basic items.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text is associated with the patronage of Jainism by the Pandya king Nedumchezhiyan?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai, a Buddhist epic by Sittalai Sattanar, reflects the religious milieu of the Sangam age, including Jain and Buddhist influences under Pandya rule.

questions.question: The 'Mahayana' school of Buddhism introduced the concept of:
questions.explanation: Mahayana Buddhism emphasized the Bodhisattva ideal—one who delays nirvana to help others—unlike the earlier Hinayana focus on individual Arhatship.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for his conversion from Jainism to Hinduism under the influence of the Shaiva saint Appar?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (600–630 CE) initially followed Jainism but later converted to Shaivism after being influenced by the Nayanar saint Appar.

questions.question: The 'Jataka' tales are associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: Jataka tales are stories about the previous births of Gautama Buddha, forming part of Buddhist canonical literature.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of Jain Tirthankaras is correctly matched with their symbols?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (Adinatha), the first Tirthankara, is symbolized by a bull; Mahavira's symbol is a lion, Parshvanatha's is a snake, and Neminatha's is a conch.

questions.question: The 'Third Buddhist Council' held at Pataliputra was convened under the patronage of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Third Buddhist Council was held under Ashoka's patronage around 250 BCE, presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa, to purify the Sangha.

questions.question: Which Jain Tirthankara is associated with the emblem of a lion and is considered the first Tirthankara of the present Avasarpini era?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (Adinatha) is the first Tirthankara; his emblem is the lion.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, which led to the formal split into Hinayana and Mahayana, was held during the reign of which ruler?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Kushana king Kanishka in Kashmir.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text provides evidence of Jain influence in ancient Tamil Nadu, mentioning the practice of Sallekhana?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai, a Buddhist epic, also reflects Jain ideas; Sallekhana is a Jain practice of fasting unto death.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for his conversion from Jainism to Shaivism under the influence of the saint Appar?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I, initially a Jain, converted to Shaivism after being influenced by the Nayanar saint Appar.

questions.question: Which Chola king is credited with building the Brihadeeswarar Temple at Thanjavur, and also persecuted Jains according to some accounts?
questions.explanation: Rajaraja I built the Brihadeeswarar Temple; his reign saw a decline of Jainism due to royal patronage of Shaivism.

questions.question: Which Jain council, held at Vallabhi in the 5th century CE, is known for the final compilation of the Jain canonical texts (Agamas)?
questions.explanation: The Second Jain Council at Vallabhi (c. 453 CE) under Devardhigani Kshamashramana finalized the Jain Agamas.

questions.question: The 'Sangam' literature term 'Aruvaththuvar' refers to which group associated with Jainism in early Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: Aruvaththuvar (the sixty-three) refers to a group of Jain monks who spread Jainism in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is credited with the propagation of Buddhism to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia?
questions.explanation: Ashoka sent missionaries, including his son Mahinda and daughter Sanghamitta, to Sri Lanka and other regions to spread Buddhism.

questions.question: The term 'Sallekhana' in Jainism refers to which practice?
questions.explanation: Sallekhana is a Jain practice of voluntary, gradual fasting to the point of death, considered a peaceful end of life.

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text is associated with the patronage of Jainism by the Pandya king Nedunchezhiyan?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai, a Buddhist epic by Sittalai Sattanar, reflects the religious milieu of the Sangam period, including Jain and Buddhist influences under Pandya rule.

questions.question: The 'Mahavamsa', a historical chronicle, is associated with which branch of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: The Mahavamsa is a Theravada Buddhist chronicle from Sri Lanka, detailing the history of Buddhism on the island.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for his patronage of Jainism before converting to Shaivism under the influence of Appar?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was initially a patron of Jainism but later converted to Shaivism, influenced by the Nayanar saint Appar.

questions.question: The 'Kalinga War' is historically significant for Buddhism because it led to which event?
options: The split of Buddhism into Mahayana and Hinayana
The spread of Buddhism to China
questions.explanation: The Kalinga War (c. 261 BCE) caused immense suffering, leading Ashoka to embrace Buddhism and adopt a policy of Dhamma.

questions.question: The first Jain Council was held at which location?
questions.explanation: The first Jain Council was held at Pataliputra in the 3rd century BCE under the patronage of Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.question: Which Buddhist council is associated with the formal split into Mahayana and Hinayana?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council, held in Kashmir under Kanishka, marked the formal split between Mahayana and Hinayana schools.

questions.question: Rishabhanatha, the first Tirthankara of Jainism, is also known by which name?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha is also called Adinatha, meaning 'first lord', and is considered the founder of Jainism in the present cosmic cycle.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is said to have embraced Jainism and later starved to death at Shravanabelagola?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya, after renouncing his throne, became a Jain monk and died by sallekhana (fasting unto death) at Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: The Buddhist Tripitaka (Three Baskets) were originally composed in which language?
questions.explanation: The Tripitaka were originally composed and preserved in Pali, the language of the Theravada Buddhist canon.

questions.question: Which famous Tamil Jain poet authored the epic 'Jivaka Chintamani'?
questions.explanation: Tiruttakkadevar, a Jain monk, wrote 'Jivaka Chintamani', one of the five great Tamil epics, during the Pallava period.

questions.question: A key difference between the Svetambara and Digambara sects of Jainism is that Digambaras believe:
questions.explanation: Digambaras hold that monks must be naked (sky-clad) as a symbol of non-attachment, while Svetambaras wear white robes.

questions.question: The Buddha delivered his first sermon at Sarnath. This event is known as:
questions.explanation: The first sermon, the Dhammachakkappavattana Sutta (Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dharma), was given at Sarnath to the five ascetics.

questions.question: Which ancient Tamil port city was a major center of Buddhism and had a famous Buddhist monastery?
questions.explanation: Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar), the Chola port, had a renowned Buddhist monastery and was a hub for Buddhist learning in Sangam times.

questions.question: During the Vijayanagara period, Jainism received patronage from which ruler known for his religious tolerance?
questions.explanation: Krishnadevaraya patronized Jainism along with other faiths, building Jain temples and granting land to Jain scholars.

questions.question: Which of the following terms is associated with Jainism and refers to the five great vows?
questions.explanation: Pancha Mahavrata are the five great vows of Jainism (non-violence, truth, non-stealing, celibacy, non-attachment). Pancha Shila are Buddhist precepts.

questions.question: The first Buddhist council was held at which place?
questions.explanation: The first Buddhist council was held at Rajagriha (modern Rajgir) under the patronage of King Ajatashatru, shortly after Buddha's death.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic is associated with Jainism and is one of the five great epics of Tamil literature?
questions.explanation: Civaka Cintamani is a Jain epic written by Tiruttakka Devar. Silappadikaram and Manimekalai are associated with Buddhism and Jain influences respectively.

questions.question: The concept of 'Sunyata' (emptiness) is central to which school of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Sunyata is a key concept in Mahayana Buddhism, particularly in the Madhyamaka school founded by Nagarjuna.

questions.question: In which region of Tamil Nadu did Jainism find significant patronage during the post-Mauryan period?
questions.explanation: The Madurai region, especially Jain caves at Sittannavasal and Kalugumalai, indicates Jain patronage under Pandya kings.

questions.question: The Buddhist equivalent of the Jain Triratna (three jewels) is called?
questions.explanation: Trisharana (Three Refuges) in Buddhism refers to Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha, similar to the Jain Triratna (Right Faith, Knowledge, Conduct).

questions.question: Which of the following Mauryan emperors is known to have embraced Jainism?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya converted to Jainism and spent his last years as a Jain monk at Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: The Buddhist text 'Milinda Panha' is a dialogue between King Menander and which Buddhist sage?
questions.explanation: Milinda Panha records the conversation between Indo-Greek king Menander (Milinda) and Buddhist monk Nagasena.

questions.question: Which Sangam age Tamil king is credited with building a Buddhist vihara at Puhar?
questions.explanation: Karikala Chola is believed to have built a Buddhist vihara at Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar) as mentioned in Silappadikaram.

questions.question: The concept of 'Ahimsa' (non-violence) is a core tenet of both Jainism and Buddhism. Which practice in Jainism is considered the strictest form of Ahimsa?
questions.explanation: Sallekhana is the Jain practice of fasting unto death, considered the ultimate expression of non-violence towards one's own body.

questions.question: At which Jain council were the canonical texts (Agamas) finally compiled?
questions.explanation: The Second Jain Council at Vallabhi (c. 5th century CE) finalized the Jain Agamas.

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Syadvada' (conditional predication) is associated with which tradition?
questions.explanation: Syadvada is a Jain doctrine that expresses truth from multiple viewpoints.

questions.question: Which Buddhist council was held at Rajagriha under the patronage of King Ajatashatru?
questions.explanation: The First Buddhist Council was convened at Rajagriha shortly after the Buddha's death.

questions.question: Which Jain saint accompanied Chandragupta Maurya to Sravana Belgola?
questions.explanation: According to tradition, Bhadrabahu led the migration of Jains to the south with Chandragupta Maurya.

questions.question: In Buddhism, the concept of 'Anatta' refers to:
questions.explanation: Anatta is the Buddhist doctrine of 'no-self' or absence of permanent soul.

questions.question: Which Jain Tirthankara is mentioned in the Rigveda?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (Adinatha), the first Tirthankara, is believed to be referenced in the Rigveda.

questions.question: The Buddhist chronicle 'Mahavamsa' was composed in which language?
questions.explanation: Mahavamsa is a Pali historical poem of Sri Lanka.

questions.question: The Pallava king who initially patronized Jainism and later converted to Saivism is:
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was a Jain patron before adopting Saivism under the influence of Appar.

questions.question: The Buddhist site Nagarjunakonda is associated with which school of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Nagarjunakonda was a major center of Mahayana Buddhism in the Ikshvaku period.

questions.question: Which of the following is a famous Jain center in Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal in Pudukkottai district is known for its Jain caves and paintings.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct chronological order of the four major Buddhist councils?
questions.explanation: The First Council was at Rajgir (c. 483 BCE), Second at Vaishali (c. 383 BCE), Third at Pataliputra (c. 250 BCE), and Fourth at Kashmir (c. 1st century CE).

questions.question: The concept of 'Triratna' (Three Jewels) in Jainism consists of Right Faith, Right Knowledge, and which of the following?
questions.explanation: The Triratna of Jainism comprises Samyak Darshana (Right Faith), Samyak Jnana (Right Knowledge), and Samyak Charitra (Right Conduct).

questions.question: Which Tamil Sangam text is considered the earliest known work to mention the Jain monk and philosopher Kundakunda?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, a Tamil epic by Ilango Adigal (a Jain prince), mentions Kundakunda, a prominent Jain acharya.

questions.question: The Kalinga War (261 BCE) had a profound impact on which ruler, leading him to embrace Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Emperor Ashoka, after witnessing the devastation of the Kalinga War, converted to Buddhism and adopted a policy of Dhamma.

questions.question: Which Pallava ruler is known for his patronage of Jainism and for building the Jain cave temples at Sittannavasal?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (c. 600–630 CE) initially patronized Jainism and built the Sittannavasal cave temple before converting to Shaivism.

questions.question: The term 'Digambara' in Jainism refers to those who practice which of the following?
questions.explanation: Digambara ('sky-clad') monks go naked as a symbol of non-possession, while Svetambara monks wear white clothes.

questions.question: Which Vijayanagara ruler is known for his tolerant religious policy and for granting land to both Buddhist and Jain monasteries?
questions.explanation: Deva Raya II (c. 1422–1446 CE) was known for religious tolerance and patronized Jain and Buddhist institutions alongside Hindu temples.

questions.question: The Buddhist concept of 'Sunyata' (Emptiness) is most closely associated with which school of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Sunyata is a core doctrine of Mahayana Buddhism, particularly emphasized by the Madhyamaka school founded by Nagarjuna.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of Jain Tirthankaras is correctly matched with their respective symbols?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (Adinatha), the first Tirthankara, is symbolized by the bull. Mahavira's symbol is the lion.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, held in Kashmir during the reign of Kanishka, resulted in the formal split of Buddhism into which two major schools?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Council under Kanishka led to the formal emergence of Mahayana Buddhism as distinct from the orthodox Theravada (Hinayana) school.

questions.question: Which Chola emperor granted land for the construction of the Buddhist vihara known as Chudamani Vihara at Nagapattinam?
questions.explanation: Rajaraja I granted the land to the Srivijaya king Maravijayottungavarman to build the Chudamani Vihara at Nagapattinam.

questions.question: The Jain council at Vallabhi (5th century CE) is primarily associated with the final codification of which Jain scripture?
questions.explanation: The Vallabhi council, under Svetambara tradition, finalized the 12 Angas, the canonical scriptures of Jainism.

questions.question: The Buddhist philosopher Nagarjuna, associated with Nalanda, founded which school of Mahayana Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Nagarjuna founded the Madhyamaka (Middle Way) school, emphasizing the doctrine of emptiness (Shunyata).

questions.question: The Pallava king Mahendravarman I was originally a follower of which faith before converting to Shaivism?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was a patron of Jainism and built Jain caves, but later converted to Shaivism under the influence of Appar.

questions.question: In Jainism, the term 'Triratna' or 'Three Jewels' refers to which set of principles?
options: Right faith, right knowledge, right conduct
questions.explanation: Triratna in Jainism consists of Samyak Darshana (right faith), Samyak Jnana (right knowledge), and Samyak Charitra (right conduct).

questions.question: The Buddhist text 'Milinda Panha' is a dialogue between King Menander and which Buddhist monk?
questions.explanation: The Milinda Panha records the conversation between the Indo-Greek king Milinda (Menander) and the monk Nagasena.

questions.question: Which Jain rock-cut cave site in Tamil Nadu is renowned for its frescoes and paintings?
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal in Tamil Nadu features ancient Jain rock-cut caves with well-preserved frescoes, including arhats and lotus ponds.

questions.question: The third Buddhist council, held at Pataliputra under the patronage of Ashoka, is primarily known for which outcome?
questions.explanation: The third council under Moggaliputta Tissa compiled the Tripitaka and expelled heretical monks, strengthening the Theravada tradition.

questions.question: The Tamil epic 'Silappadikaram' was composed by which Jain poet?
questions.explanation: Ilango Adigal, a Jain monk and brother of the Chera king Senguttuvan, authored the epic Silappadikaram.

questions.question: The Buddhist monk who led the first mission to introduce Buddhism to Sri Lanka was?
questions.explanation: Mahinda, son of Ashoka, was sent to Sri Lanka where he converted King Devanampiya Tissa and established the Sangha.

questions.question: Which of the following is considered the first Tirthankara of Jainism?
questions.explanation: Rishabhanatha (also known as Adinatha) is regarded as the first Tirthankara in Jain tradition.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is credited with patronizing the Third Buddhist Council held at Pataliputra?
questions.explanation: The Third Buddhist Council was convened under the patronage of Emperor Ashoka at Pataliputra around 250 BCE.

questions.question: The term 'Sangha' in Buddhism primarily refers to which of the following?
questions.explanation: In Buddhism, Sangha refers to the monastic community of bhikkhus (monks) and bhikkhunis (nuns).

questions.question: The famous rock-cut caves at Udayagiri and Khandagiri in Odisha are associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: The Udayagiri and Khandagiri caves were built by King Kharavela of the Mahameghavahana dynasty and are predominantly Jain.

questions.question: Which of the following Buddhist texts is a part of the Sutta Pitaka and contains the Buddha's first sermon?
questions.explanation: The Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta (Setting in Motion the Wheel of Dharma) records the Buddha's first sermon at Sarnath.

questions.question: The 'Jataka' tales are associated with which of the following?
questions.explanation: Jataka tales are stories about the previous births of Gautama Buddha, illustrating moral lessons.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for building the famous 'Shore Temple' at Mamallapuram, a UNESCO World Heritage site?
questions.explanation: The Shore Temple at Mamallapuram was built by Narasimhavarman I (also known as Mamalla) of the Pallava dynasty.

questions.question: The 'Sangam' age in Tamil Nadu is roughly contemporary with which of the following periods in North Indian history?
questions.explanation: The Sangam age (c. 300 BCE – 300 CE) overlaps with the post-Mauryan and early Gupta periods in North India.

questions.question: Which Jain saint is credited with composing the 'Kalpa Sutra', a key text detailing the lives of the Tirthankaras?
questions.explanation: Bhadrabahu is traditionally credited with composing the Kalpa Sutra, though it was later compiled by Devardhigani Kshamashramana.

questions.question: The famous Buddhist monastery at Nagapattinam, visited by the Chinese traveller Hiuen Tsang, was built during the reign of which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Chola king Rajaraja I built the Buddhist monastery at Nagapattinam, known as the 'Chudamani Vihara'.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is associated with the propagation of Jainism, and is said to have starved himself to death at Shravanabelagola?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya, in his later years, became a Jain monk and followed Bhadrabahu to Shravanabelagola, where he fasted unto death.

questions.question: The 'Sangam' text 'Manimekalai' is primarily associated with which religious tradition?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai, written by Sittalai Sattanar, is a Buddhist epic that narrates the story of a Buddhist nun.

questions.question: Which Pallava king is known for his persecution of Jainism and his subsequent conversion back to Hinduism under the influence of the saint Appar?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I, initially a Jain, was converted to Shaivism by Appar and is known for his work 'Mattavilasa Prahasana' mocking Buddhist and Jain monks.

questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between the Hinayana and Mahayana schools of Buddhism?
options: Hinayana believes in idol worship; Mahayana does not
Mahayana emphasizes Bodhisattva ideal; Hinayana emphasizes Arhat ideal
Hinayana uses Sanskrit; Mahayana uses Pali
Mahayana rejects the Four Noble Truths; Hinayana accepts them
questions.explanation: Mahayana focuses on the Bodhisattva path (seeking enlightenment for all), while Hinayana (Theravada) focuses on individual Arhatship.

questions.question: The 'Third Buddhist Council' held at Pataliputra was convened under the patronage of which emperor?
questions.explanation: The Third Buddhist Council was convened by Emperor Ashoka at Pataliputra under the presidency of Moggaliputta Tissa.

questions.question: Which Jain council is associated with the final compilation of the Jain scriptures (Angas) at Vallabhi?
questions.explanation: The Second Council of Vallabhi (5th century CE), under Devardhigani, compiled the Jain scriptures into their present form.

questions.question: In Buddhism, the term 'Triratna' refers to:
questions.explanation: Triratna (Three Jewels) in Buddhism are Buddha (the enlightened), Dhamma (the teachings), and Sangha (the monastic community).

questions.question: Which Pallava king initially patronized Jainism but later converted to Shaivism under the influence of Saint Appar?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I (7th century CE) was a Jain patron before being converted to Shaivism by the Nayanmar Appar.

questions.question: The Jain rock-cut cave temple at Sittannavasal (Pudukkottai) is famous for:
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal is known for its Jain cave temple with well-preserved fresco paintings and a natural pond called 'Samanar Palli'.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most authentic source of information about Ashoka's policies and conversion to Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's rock edicts, inscribed on pillars and rocks, directly record his policies, conversion, and propagation of Dhamma.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, held under the patronage of Kanishka, took place at:
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council was convened by Kanishka at Kundalavana in Kashmir, leading to the split into Hinayana and Mahayana.

questions.question: During the Kalabhra interregnum in Tamil Nadu, which religions received significant royal patronage?
questions.explanation: The Kalabhras (3rd–6th century CE) were patrons of Buddhism and Jainism, leading to a decline in Hindu influence.

questions.question: In Jainism, the 'Angas' refer to:
questions.explanation: The 12 Angas form the core of Jain canonical literature, originally compiled at the Vallabhi council.

questions.question: Which Tamil Nadu site is known for a large brick Buddhist stupa and a historic vihara?
questions.explanation: Nagapattinam's Buddhist site (known as 'Palli') has a massive brick stupa and was a major center of Buddhism in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly distinguishes Jainism from Buddhism regarding the principle of Ahimsa (non-violence)?
options: Jainism extends extreme non-violence to all life (including plants and microbes); Buddhism allows limited exceptions
Buddhism rejects the concept of karma; Jainism accepts it
Jainism does not practice asceticism; Buddhism does
Both believe in a creator god
questions.explanation: Jainism observes strict non-violence towards all living beings, while Buddhism permits certain exceptions (e.g., eating meat if not killed specifically for the eater).

questions.question: The Jain text 'Tattvartha Sutra' was composed by which Acharya?
questions.explanation: Umashvati (Umaswati) authored the Tattvartha Sutra, a foundational Jain philosophical text.

questions.question: The Buddhist concept of 'Sangha' refers to:
questions.explanation: Sangha is the monastic community of Buddhist monks and nuns, one of the Three Jewels.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic is a Buddhist work, written by Sithalai Sattanar?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai is a Tamil Buddhist epic by Sithalai Sattanar, continuing the story from Silappadikaram.

questions.question: The Fourth Buddhist Council, which led to the split into Hinayana and Mahayana, was held under the patronage of:
questions.explanation: Kanishka convened the Fourth Buddhist Council in Kashmir, where Mahayana Buddhism formally emerged.

questions.question: The practice of 'Sallekhana' in Jainism refers to:
questions.explanation: Sallekhana is the Jain practice of voluntarily fasting to death, considered a form of spiritual detachment.

questions.question: Which Chinese pilgrim visited Kanchipuram during the Pallava period and recorded Buddhist presence?
questions.explanation: Hiuen Tsang (Xuanzang) visited Kanchipuram in the 7th century and noted many Buddhist monasteries there.

questions.question: The famous Jain temple of 'Sittannavasal' is located in which state?
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal, a Jain rock-cut temple with ancient frescoes, is in Pudukkottai district, Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: The term 'Triratna' in Buddhism refers to:
questions.explanation: Triratna or Three Jewels are Buddha, Dharma (teachings), and Sangha (community), the core of Buddhism.

questions.question: Which Mauryan emperor is known for spreading Buddhism and building stupas?
questions.explanation: Ashoka embraced Buddhism after Kalinga war and propagated it through edicts, stupas, and missions.

questions.question: The Jain council at Valabhi was convened in the 5th century CE under the patronage of:
questions.explanation: King Dhruvasena of the Maitraka dynasty convened the Valabhi council to compile the Jain Siddhanta.

questions.question: Which Jain council, held in the 5th century CE, is credited with the final compilation of the Jain canonical literature (Siddhanta)?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Jain Council at Valabhi (c. 453-467 CE) under Devardhigani Kshamashramana systematically compiled and wrote down the Jain scriptures (Siddhanta).

questions.question: The Buddhist monk Nagasena, known for his debates with the Indo-Greek king Menander, is associated with which text?
questions.explanation: Milindapanha (Questions of Milinda) records the philosophical dialogue between King Menander (Milinda) and the monk Nagasena on Buddhist doctrines.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between Jainism and Buddhism in the Tamil region during the early historic period is correct?
options: Both religions were patronized exclusively by the Chera and Chola kings, leading to suppression of Hinduism.
Jainism received royal patronage from the Kalabhras, while Buddhism flourished under the later Pallavas.
Both faiths declined sharply after the Bhakti movement, but Buddhism survived longer in Tamil Nadu due to its monastic structures.
Jainism was more widespread in the Tamil region during the Sangam age, whereas Buddhism arrived only during the post-Sangam period.
questions.explanation: The Kalabhra rulers (3rd–6th century CE) were patrons of Jainism, while the later Pallavas supported Buddhism, as seen in sites like Kanchipuram.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Gupta period and its relation to Buddhism and Jainism:
options: The Guptas were exclusively followers of Buddhism, leading to the decline of Jainism.
Buddhist monasteries received state grants, but Jainism was actively suppressed by Samudragupta.
Both Buddhism and Jainism flourished under the Guptas, with Nalanda becoming a major Buddhist centre and Jainism retaining royal support in certain regions.
The Gupta period saw the complete replacement of Buddhism by Jainism in eastern India.
questions.explanation: The Gupta period was a golden age for both; Nalanda university was a Buddhist stronghold, while Jainism continued in western and southern regions.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the role of langal (plough) and vimana (sanctum) in the context of Tamil Buddhism and Jainism is correct?
options: The langal symbol was used exclusively by Jain monastic orders to denote agriculture, while the vimana was a Buddhist architectural feature.
The langal (plough) is a symbol associated with Jain Tirthankara Neminatha, and the vimana concept in temple architecture was later adopted by Buddhists for stupa structures.
There is no connection between langal and vimana in either religion.
The langal symbol appears on early Buddhist coins from Tamil Nadu, and vimana is a Jain term for mountain caves.
questions.explanation: Neminatha, the 22nd Jain Tirthankara, is often depicted with a plough; vimana refers to the tower over the garbhagriha, later adopted in Buddhist architecture for multi-tiered stupas.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of Tamil literary works and their religious affiliation is correctly matched?
options: Silappadikaram – Jain epic; Manimekalai – Buddhist epic
Silappadikaram – Buddhist epic; Manimekalai – Jain epic
Silappadikaram and Manimekalai are both Jain works
Silappadikaram and Manimekalai are both Buddhist works
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram has a Jain framework (author Ilango Adigal was a Jain prince), while Manimekalai is a Buddhist epic (author Sattanar was a Buddhist).

questions.question: With reference to the socio-religious impact of Jainism and Buddhism in the Tamil region, which statement is correct?
options: Both religions introduced the concept of ahimsa, which led to the complete cessation of animal sacrifices in Tamil society.
Jainism promoted the idea of varnashrama dharma, while Buddhism opposed it.
Buddhism appealed more to the merchant class, leading to a network of trade guilds, while Jainism gained a foothold among the priestly class.
Both religions contributed to the growth of Tamil literature and architecture, but Buddhism declined more rapidly after the Bhakti movement.
questions.explanation: Both enriched Tamil literature (e.g., Jain contributions to grammar, Buddhist works like Manimekalai); Buddhism declined sharply after the Bhakti revival, while Jainism survived in pockets.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Buddhist and Jain councils is correct?
options: The First Buddhist Council was held at Rajgriha under the patronage of Bimbisara, and the first Jain council was at Pataliputra under Chandragupta Maurya.
The Fourth Buddhist Council (Kashmir) was convened by Kanishka, and the Jain council at Vallabhi fixed the Jain canon in the 5th century CE.
Both religions held their first councils at the same location (Pataliputra).
The Jain council at Shravana Belgola standardized the Digambara texts in the 3rd century BCE.
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council under Kanishka (1st–2nd century CE) produced the Mahayana canon; the Jain council at Vallabhi (5th century CE) compiled the Svetambara scriptures.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Vijayanagara Empire's attitude towards Buddhism and Jainism:
options: The Vijayanagara rulers were exclusively Vaishnavas and persecuted both Jain and Buddhist communities.
Buddhism had become extinct in South India by the Vijayanagara period, while Jainism enjoyed royal patronage, especially under Bukka Raya I.
Both Buddhism and Jainism were actively suppressed by Krishnadevaraya.
Jainism was completely absent in the Vijayanagara kingdom, while Buddhism had limited presence in coastal areas.
questions.explanation: Buddhism had largely vanished from the Deccan by the 14th century; Jainism continued to receive patronage, such as from Bukka Raya I who built a Jain temple.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies the role of the Sangam and post-Sangam period in the development of Buddhism and Jainism in Tamil Nadu?
options: Sangam literature makes no mention of Buddhism or Jainism, indicating their absence in Tamil society.
Jainism was introduced during the Sangam period by the emperor Ashoka, while Buddhism arrived only in the 5th century CE.
Both religions were present in the Sangam period, and post-Sangam works like Tolkappiyam and Tirukkural show their influence.
Buddhism was dominant in the Sangam age, but Jainism replaced it completely by the post-Sangam period.
questions.explanation: Tolkappiyam mentions Jain monks; Tirukkural's ethical content shows Buddhist/Jain influences; both faiths coexisted in the Tamil region from early times.

questions.question: With reference to the Maratha rule in Tamil Nadu (such as under the Thanjavur Marathas), which statement about Buddhism and Jainism is correct?
options: The Marathas were patrons of Jainism, building many Jain temples in Tamil Nadu.
Buddhism revived in Tamil Nadu during the Maratha period due to trade links with Southeast Asia.
Jainism continued as a minority community, but Buddhism had completely disappeared from the region by then.
Both religions were forcibly converted to Hinduism under the Maratha administration.
questions.explanation: Jainism survived among merchant communities; Buddhism had ceased to exist in Tamil Nadu by the 13th century, and the Maratha period (17th–18th century) saw no Buddhist revival.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the British colonial period and its impact on Jainism and Buddhism in Tamil Nadu:
options: The British actively promoted Jainism by granting land to Jain monastic orders.
Buddhism was revived in Tamil Nadu in the 19th century through the efforts of Anagarika Dharmapala, leading to many conversions.
Jainism declined rapidly during the British period due to the imposition of Western education, while Buddhism grew.
The British census recorded Jain communities only in the western districts of Tamil Nadu, while Buddhism was virtually absent.
questions.explanation: Census records show Jain populations concentrated in districts like Dharmapuri and Salem; Buddhism had no significant presence in Tamil Nadu by the British period.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding the Mauryan emperor Ashoka's policy towards non-Buddhist sects: 1. Ashoka's Dhamma was a syncretic policy that promoted tolerance towards all sects including Jainism. 2. Ashoka explicitly banned animal sacrifices, a practice endorsed by Vedic Brahmanism but opposed by Jainism and Buddhism. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Ashoka's Dhamma promoted tolerance (statement 1 is correct), and his edicts banned animal sacrifices (statement 2 is correct), aligning with Jain/Buddhist principles.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the difference between the Jain concept of 'Anekantavada' and the Buddhist concept of 'Madhyamaka'?
options: Anekantavada denies the existence of the soul, while Madhyamaka affirms it.
Anekantavada emphasizes multiple viewpoints of reality, while Madhyamaka focuses on the middle path of emptiness.
Both are identical doctrines of non-violence.
Anekantavada is a political theory, while Madhyamaka is a metaphysical theory.
questions.explanation: Anekantavada (Jainism) holds that reality can be perceived from multiple viewpoints, while Madhyamaka (Buddhism) is the doctrine of the middle way and emptiness (Shunyata).

questions.question: During the Vijayanagara Empire, which of the following best describes the state's relationship with Jainism and Buddhism?
options: Both religions were actively persecuted, leading to their complete disappearance.
Jainism received significant patronage, especially from the Sangama dynasty, while Buddhism had negligible presence.
Buddhism was revived under Krishnadevaraya, while Jainism was suppressed.
Both religions were merged into a single syncretic faith under the empire.
questions.explanation: The Vijayanagara rulers (especially early Sangama dynasty) patronized Jainism, building temples and granting land, but Buddhism had largely declined in the region by then.

questions.question: The 'Third Buddhist Council' held during the reign of Ashoka is significant because it:
options: Led to the compilation of the Vinaya Pitaka and the expulsion of heretical monks.
Resulted in the formal split of Buddhism into Mahayana and Hinayana.
Was convened to translate Buddhist texts into Tamil.
Established the first monastic order for women.
questions.explanation: The Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra (c. 250 BCE) compiled the Vinaya Pitaka and expelled non-conformist monks, but the Mahayana-Hinayana split occurred later.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Jain council held at Vallabhi (c. 5th century CE) is correct?
options: It resulted in the final codification of the Jain canon (Agamas) in the Svetambara tradition.
It led to the permanent schism between Svetambara and Digambara sects.
It was convened under the patronage of the Gupta emperor Chandragupta II.
It focused on translating Jain texts into Pali.
questions.explanation: The Vallabhi council (c. 453-467 CE) under the Svetambara tradition finalized the written compilation of the Jain Agamas; the Digambara-Svetambara split predates this.

questions.question: In the context of the Tamil Sangam age, which of the following is a correct observation about the influence of Jainism and Buddhism?
options: Both religions were completely absent from Sangam literature.
Jain and Buddhist monks contributed to the development of Tamil grammar and literature, such as the Tolkappiyam.
The Sangam polity was exclusively Vedic, rejecting all heterodox sects.
Buddhism was the state religion of the early Cholas.
questions.explanation: Jain and Buddhist scholars (like the author of Tolkappiyam, traditionally associated with Jainism) significantly influenced early Tamil grammar and literary works.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the 'Sangha' in early Buddhism as distinct from the Jain 'Sangha'?
options: The Buddhist Sangha was exclusively for monks, while the Jain Sangha included lay followers.
The Buddhist Sangha admitted women from its inception, while the Jain Sangha initially did not.
The Buddhist Sangha was a democratic body with voting, while the Jain Sangha was authoritarian.
The Buddhist Sangha was primarily a political organization, while the Jain Sangha was purely spiritual.
questions.explanation: Buddha admitted women into the Sangha (order of nuns) from his lifetime, while Jainism's Digambara sect traditionally denied women the possibility of liberation.

questions.question: The concept of 'Ahimsa' (non-violence) in Jainism is considered more rigorous than in Buddhism because:
options: Jainism prohibits only intentional killing, while Buddhism prohibits all forms of killing.
Jainism extends non-violence to all living beings including microorganisms, while Buddhism focuses on sentient beings.
Jainism allows violence in self-defense, while Buddhism forbids it absolutely.
Buddhism permits animal sacrifice in certain rituals, while Jainism does not.
questions.explanation: Jainism's doctrine of Ahimsa is absolute, extending to all life forms including invisible microbes (nigodas), while Buddhism's emphasis is on sentient beings and intent (cetana).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the decline of Buddhism in India during the early medieval period is most accurate?
options: It was primarily due to the invasion of Huns and the destruction of monasteries.
It was caused by the rise of the Gupta empire which exclusively patronized Vedic Hinduism.
It resulted from a combination of the Bhakti movement, loss of royal patronage, and the monastic focus on logic over lay needs.
It was because Buddhism merged entirely with Jainism to form a single faith.
questions.explanation: The decline of Buddhism was multi-causal: the Bhakti movement offered a devotional alternative, royal patronage shifted to Hindu sects, and Buddhist monasteries became overly scholastic.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the first Jain council is correct?
options: It was held at Pataliputra under the patronage of Chandragupta Maurya
It resulted in the compilation of the 12 Angas and the split into Svetambara and Digambara
It was presided over by Mahavira's disciple Sudharman
It led to the final canonization of Jain scriptures in Ardhamagadhi
questions.explanation: The first Jain council at Pataliputra (c. 300 BCE) compiled the 12 Angas and formalized the Svetambara-Digambara schism.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about the Fourth Buddhist Council: (1) It was held under the patronage of Kanishka. (2) It resulted in the division of Buddhism into Hinayana and Mahayana. (3) The commentaries were written in Pali. Which are correct?
questions.explanation: The Fourth Buddhist Council under Kanishka (c. 1st-2nd cent. CE) led to the Mahayana-Hinayana split; commentaries were in Sanskrit, not Pali.

questions.question: Which Tamil king is traditionally associated with the patronage of Jainism and the composition of the epic 'Silappadikaram' by his brother Ilango Adigal?
questions.explanation: Ilango Adigal, a Jain prince, wrote Silappadikaram; his brother Senguttuvan Chera (2nd cent. CE) is the patron king mentioned in the epic.

questions.question: The concept of 'Anatta' (no-soul) in Buddhism directly contradicts which core Jain doctrine?
questions.explanation: Jainism believes in eternal individual souls (jiva), while Buddhism's Anatta denies any permanent self, directly opposing the Jain jiva concept.

questions.question: Which Mauryan ruler is known to have embraced Jainism and spent his last days as a Jain ascetic at Shravanabelagola?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya, according to Jain tradition, abdicated, became a Jain monk, and fasted to death at Shravanabelagola.

questions.question: The Buddhist text 'Mahavastu' belongs to which school of Buddhism and is written in which language?
questions.explanation: Mahavastu is a text of the Mahasanghika (Lokottaravada) school, written in a mixture of Sanskrit and Prakrit.

questions.question: Which of the following Tamil literary works is attributed to a Jain author and is considered part of the 'Patinenkilkanakku' anthology?
questions.explanation: Naladiyar, a collection of 400 verses on ethics, is attributed to Jain ascetics and belongs to the Patinenkilkanakku.

questions.question: The decline of Buddhism in India was accelerated by the efforts of which philosopher who revived Hinduism and criticized Buddhist doctrines?
questions.explanation: Shankaracharya (8th cent. CE) revived Advaita Vedanta, debated Buddhist scholars, and contributed to the decline of Buddhism in India.

questions.question: Which ancient port city in Tamil Nadu is known for its Buddhist vihara and was a major center of Buddhism under the Pallavas and Cholas?
questions.explanation: Nagapattinam had a famous Buddhist vihara (Chudamani Vihara) built by the Srivijaya king and patronized by Pallava and Chola rulers.

questions.question: Which Jain rock-cut cave temple in Tamil Nadu is famous for its frescoes and is associated with the Pandya king Nedunjeliyan?
questions.explanation: Sittannavasal (Pudukkottai) contains Jain cave temples with 7th-century frescoes, linked to the Pandya king Nedunjeliyan.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the First Buddhist Council is historically accurate?
options: It was held at Rajgriha under King Bimbisara's patronage
It was presided by Mahakashyapa to codify the Buddha's teachings
It was convened at Vaishali to resolve Vinaya disputes
It led to the formal schism between Hinayana and Mahayana
questions.explanation: The First Council was held at Rajgriha after Buddha's death, presided by Mahakashyapa and patronized by King Ajatashatru, not Bimbisara.

questions.question: Consider the following pairs: (a) Syadvada – Jainism, (b) Anatta – Buddhism, (c) Anekantavada – Jainism. Which of these is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Syadvada (theory of conditional predication) and Anekantavada (non-absolutism) are Jain doctrines, while Anatta (no-self) is a core Buddhist concept.

questions.question: The Third Buddhist Council, held during Ashoka’s reign, is known for the compilation of which text?
questions.explanation: The Third Council (c. 250 BCE) at Pataliputra produced the Kathavatthu, authored by Moggaliputta Tissa, to refute heretical views.

questions.question: Which Tamil epic, traditionally attributed to the Jain prince Ilango Adigal, is considered a classic of Jain literature?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram, composed by Ilango Adigal (a Jain ascetic), is one of the five great epics of Tamil literature and strongly reflects Jain ethics.

questions.question: The Buddhist site at Nagapattinam in Tamil Nadu is historically significant because of:
options: The Chudamani Vihara built by a Srivijaya king under Chola patronage
The Pallava king Mahendravarman’s conversion to Buddhism
The discovery of a large Buddhist monastery from the Sangam period
questions.explanation: The Chudamani Vihara was built by the Srivijaya king Maravijayottungavarman during the reign of Chola Rajaraja I.

questions.question: The Greco-Indian king Menander (Milinda) is associated with which Buddhist text that records his dialogue with the monk Nagasena?
questions.explanation: Milinda Panha (Questions of Milinda) is a Pali text documenting the conversations between King Menander and the Buddhist sage Nagasena.

questions.question: The Jain caves at Sittanavasal in Tamil Nadu are predominantly associated with which ruling dynasty?
questions.explanation: Sittanavasal's rock-cut Jain beds and frescoes were largely patronized by the Pandya kings during the 7th–9th centuries.

questions.question: Which of Ashoka’s rock edicts mentions the names of five contemporary Hellenistic kings to indicate the spread of Buddhism?
questions.explanation: Rock Edict XIII lists Antiochos, Ptolemy, Antigonos, Magas, and Alexander as recipients of Ashoka’s dhamma missions.

questions.question: Consider the following statements about Buddhism and Jainism: 1. Both rejected the authority of the Vedas. 2. Both believed in the existence of a permanent soul (Atman). 3. Both advocated non-violence (Ahimsa). Which are correct?
questions.explanation: Both rejected Vedic authority and practiced non-violence, but Buddhism denies a permanent soul (Anatta) while Jainism accepts a soul (Jiva).

questions.question: Which Tamil Buddhist epic, composed by Seethalai Sathanar, is considered a sequel to Silappadikaram and emphasizes Buddhist doctrines?
questions.explanation: Manimekalai is a Buddhist epic by Seethalai Sathanar, continuing the story of Kannagi’s daughter and promoting Buddhist ideals.

questions.question: Consider the following statements: (i) Silappadikaram is associated with Jainism. (ii) Manimekalai is a Buddhist epic. Which of the statements is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram has Jain influences and Manimekalai is a Buddhist epic, so both statements are correct.

questions.question: Which Chola king is known for patronizing Buddhism and supporting the construction of the Chulamanivihara monastery at Nagapattinam?
questions.explanation: Rajaraja I granted land for the Buddhist monastery Chulamanivihara at Nagapattinam.

questions.question: The concept of 'Sunyata' (emptiness) is central to which school of Buddhism that emerged during the Gupta period?
questions.explanation: Madhyamika, founded by Nagarjuna, emphasized the doctrine of Sunyata.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a reason for the decline of Buddhism in India?
options: Adoption of Sanskrit and Hindu rituals by Buddhists
Complete absence of royal patronage after the Gupta period
questions.explanation: The Pala dynasty and other rulers continued to patronize Buddhism after the Gupta period.

questions.question: Which Pallava king initially patronized Jainism but later converted to Shaivism and persecuted Jains?
questions.explanation: Mahendravarman I was a Jain who later adopted Shaivism and persecuted Jains.

questions.question: Which reform movement during the freedom struggle aimed at the revival of Buddhism and the restoration of Bodh Gaya?
questions.explanation: The Maha Bodhi Society, founded by Anagarika Dharmapala, worked to revive Buddhism and restore Bodh Gaya.

questions.question: The Buddhist text 'Milinda Panha' records a dialogue between King Menander and the monk Nagasena. To which period does this belong?
questions.explanation: Menander was an Indo-Greek king of the 2nd century BCE, placing the text in the Post-Mauryan Indo-Greek period.

questions.question: Consider the following pairs: (i) Kalinga war – Ashoka's conversion to Buddhism; (ii) Third Buddhist Council – Patanjali; (iii) Fourth Buddhist Council – Kanishka. Which pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Pair (i) and (iii) are correct; the Third Buddhist Council was held under Ashoka, not Patanjali.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of the Amaravati school of Buddhist art?
options: Depiction of Buddha in human form
questions.explanation: Amaravati school used greenish limestone, not white marble; Roman influence is seen in Gandhara.

questions.question: Which of the following was a prominent Buddhist center during the Sangam age in Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar) was a major port and Buddhist center during the Sangam period.

questions.question: The Jain doctrine of Anekantavada is based on the premise that reality can be perceived from multiple perspectives, leading to the theory of Syadvada. Which of the following best describes the relationship between Anekantavada and Syadvada?
options: Syadvada is the logical method of Anekantavada
Anekantavada is a subset of Syadvada
questions.explanation: Anekantavada (non-absolutism) is the ontological foundation, while Syadvada (theory of conditional predication) is the logical expression of that non-absolutism.

questions.question: The first Jain council was held at Pataliputra under the leadership of Sthulabhadra. What was the primary reason for this council?
options: To compile the Jain canon after a famine
To resolve the split into Digambara and Svetambara
